Real-World Effectiveness of Advanced Therapies in Microscopic Colitis
  • Source: Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, Vol. 23 (2026)
  • Topic: Microscopic colitis treatment with biologics and JAK inhibitors
  • Author(s): Verstockt B, Taelman T, Din S, et al. (42 authors total)
Study Overview
Research Design
  • Multinational retrospective study via ECCO CONFER network
  • Data from 15 countries and 38 centers
  • STROBE guidelines followed for reporting
  • Ethics approved at UZ/KU Leuven (S68207)
Study Population
  • 142 unique patients with microscopic colitis (MC)
  • 229 treatment cycles total (multiple lines of therapy per patient)
  • Histologic subtypes: 52.8% collagenous colitis (CC), 47.2% lymphocytic colitis (LC)
  • 85.9% female predominance
  • Median age at MC diagnosis: 52.6 years [IQR 38.3–61.7]
  • Median age at first advanced therapy: 56.6 years [IQR 44.3–65.3]
  • Median time from diagnosis to advanced therapy: 2.7 years [IQR 0.9–6.2]
Prior Treatment History
  • All but one patient had previously received budesonide
  • Reasons for budesonide discontinuation:
  • Dependency: 45.4% (n=64)
  • Secondary loss of response (LOR): 35.5% (n=50)
  • Primary non-response (PNR): 11.3% (n=16)
  • Intolerance: 7.8% (n=11)
Treatment Patterns
Drug Class Distribution Across All 229 Cycles
  • Anti-TNF agents: 55.9% (n=128)
  • Infliximab: most common first-line
  • Adalimumab: second most common
  • Vedolizumab: 28.8% (n=66)
  • JAK inhibitors: 9.2% (n=21)
  • Tofacitinib: n=9
  • Filgotitinib: n=6
  • Upadacitinib: n=6
  • Ustekinumab: 4.8% (n=11)
  • Anti-IL23 agents: 0.9% (n=2)
  • S1PR modulators: 0.4% (n=1)
Treatment Sequencing
  • First-line: predominantly anti-TNF and vedolizumab
  • Later lines (3rd–5th): ↑ use of JAK inhibitors and other agents
  • Anti-TNF remained most prescribed across all lines
Drug Exposure Duration (Median)
Drug Median Exposure
Infliximab 0.9 years [IQR 0.4–2.0]
Adalimumab 0.9 years [IQR 0.4–2.3]
Vedolizumab 0.7 years [IQR 0.3–1.5]
Ustekinumab 0.6 years [IQR 0.3–1.1]
JAK inhibitors 1.6 years [IQR 0.4–2.2]
Short-Term Drug Effectiveness
Overall Outcomes (End of Induction)
  • Clinical response: 65.1%
  • Clinical remission: 48.9%
Outcomes by Drug Class
Infliximab
  • Stool frequency ↓ from 7.0 [5.0–10.0] → 3.0 [2.0–5.8] per 24h
  • Clinical response: 68.1%
  • Clinical remission: 48.6%
  • p<0.001 for stool frequency reduction
Adalimumab
  • Stool frequency ↓ from 6.0 [5.3–8.0] → 4.0 [2.0–6.0]
  • Clinical response: 60.8%
  • Clinical remission: 51.0%
  • p<0.001 for stool frequency reduction
Vedolizumab
  • Clinical response: 59.1%
  • Clinical remission: 40.9%
  • p<0.001 for stool frequency reduction
Ustekinumab
  • Clinical response: 54.5%
  • Clinical remission: 36.4%
  • p=0.01 for stool frequency reduction
JAK Inhibitors (Best Outcomes)
  • Stool frequency ↓ from 7.0 [5.0–10.0] → 2.0 [1.5–2.0]
  • Clinical response: 95.2%
  • Clinical remission: 81.0%
  • p<0.001 for stool frequency reduction
  • Predominantly used in later treatment lines
Statistical Comparisons (vs Anti-TNF Reference)
Comparison Response OR (95% CI) Remission OR (95% CI)
JAK inhibitors 8.71 (1.70–44.74), p=0.009 4.39 (1.42–13.60), p=0.01
Vedolizumab 0.80 (0.42–1.52), p=0.50 0.73 (0.39–1.36), p=0.33
Ustekinumab 0.62 (0.17–2.21), p=0.46 0.59 (0.16–2.18), p=0.43
JAK vs Non-JAK Binary Comparison
  • Response: OR 10.0 (1.85–50.0), p=0.008
  • Remission: OR 5.0 (1.64–14.3), p=0.005
Independent Predictors of Short-Term Response
  • Drug class → only independent predictor
  • Sex, age, smoking status, histological subtype → not significant
Long-Term Drug Persistence
Discontinuation Rates by Drug
Drug Discontinuation Rate
Infliximab 62.5% (median 0.8 years)
Adalimumab 54.9% (median 0.6 years)
Vedolizumab 56.1% (median 0.5 years)
Ustekinumab 63.6% (median 0.3 years)
JAK inhibitors 23.8% (median 0.4 years)
Reasons for Discontinuation
  • Primary non-response: 45.9%
  • Secondary LOR: 28.7%
  • Adverse events/intolerance: 18.0%
  • Only 3.3% discontinued due to sustained remission (median 1.0 years)
Infliximab Discontinuation Details
  • 62.5% discontinued after median 0.8 years [IQR 0.3–1.4]
  • Causes:
  • Intolerance: n=15 (33.3%)
  • Secondary LOR: n=14 (31.1%)
  • PNR: n=13 (28.9%)
Adalimumab Discontinuation Details
  • 54.9% discontinued after median 0.6 years [IQR 0.3–1.6]
  • Causes:
  • PNR: n=16 (57.1%)
  • LOR: n=10 (35.7%)
  • Intolerance: n=1 (3.6%)
Vedolizumab Discontinuation Details
  • 56.1% discontinued after median 0.5 years [IQR 0.2–1.0]
  • Causes:
  • PNR: n=23 (62.2%)
  • LOR: n=10 (27.0%)
  • No discontinuations due to AEs
JAK Inhibitor Durability (Best)
  • Odds ratio for persistence: 4.76 (95% CI 1.64–14.3), p=0.004
  • HR for discontinuation: 0.41 (95% CI 0.21–0.82), p=0.01
  • Only discontinued due to intolerance after median 0.4 years
Cox Proportional Hazards Analysis
  • JAK inhibitors → HR 0.41 (95% CI 0.21–0.82), p=0.01
  • Marginal Cox model (robust sandwich estimators): HR 0.39 (95% CI 0.19–0.79), p=0.008
  • Results consistent across sensitivity analyses
Multivariate Analysis: Predictors of Therapy Continuation
Variable OR (95% CI) p-value
JAK inhibitors vs biologics 5.07 (1.52–16.9) 0.008
Vedolizumab vs Infliximab 1.61 (0.74–3.52) 0.232
Adalimumab vs Infliximab 1.47 (0.72–3.02) 0.294
Ustekinumab vs Infliximab 0.97 (0.26–3.59) 0.966
Age at therapy start 1.01 (0.96–1.06) 0.840
Age at MC diagnosis 1.00 (0.95–1.05) 0.946
Male sex 0.77 (0.33–1.79) 0.538
LC vs CC histology 0.85 (0.44–1.66) 0.633
Current smoking 1.68 (0.82–3.48) 0.159
Former smoking 1.05 (0.60–1.86) 0.857
  • Drug class → only independent predictor of long-term continuation
  • No demographic or clinical variables predicted outcomes
Surgical Outcomes
  • 4.2% of total cohort (n=6) required colorectal surgery
  • 83.3% had collagenous colitis
  • Indications: inadequately controlled disease
  • Procedures:
  • Proctocolectomy: n=3
  • Diverting ileostomy: n=3
Safety Profile
  • No new safety signals observed in entire cohort
  • JAK inhibitor use requires careful patient selection given:
  • VTE risk concerns (EMA warnings for elderly patients)
  • Elevated baseline VTE risk in MC patients
  • IBD patient data on JAK inhibitors show no emerging safety signals
Study Limitations
  • Retrospective design → prone to selection and reporting bias
  • Expert centers predominantly contributed → may limit generalizability
  • No systematic histological reassessment data
  • No therapeutic drug monitoring data for anti-TNFs
  • No Microscopic Colitis Score (MCS) data available
Key Findings Summary
Short-Term Effectiveness Hierarchy

1. JAK inhibitors: 95.2% response, 81.0% remission

2. Infliximab: 68.1% response, 48.6% remission

3. Adalimumab: 60.8% response, 51.0% remission

4. Vedolizumab: 59.1% response, 40.9% remission

5. Ustekinumab: 54.5% response, 36.4% remission

Long-Term Durability Hierarchy

1. JAK inhibitors: 23.8% discontinuation rate

2. Adalimumab: 54.9% discontinuation rate

3. Vedolizumab: 56.1% discontinuation rate

4. Infliximab: 62.5% discontinuation rate

5. Ustekinumab: 63.6% discontinuation rate

Clinical Implications
  • JAK inhibitors show paradigm shift potential in MC management
  • Drug class is the only independent predictor of both short-term response and long-term persistence
  • Histologic subtype (CC vs LC) does not affect outcomes
  • 4.2% ultimately require surgery despite advanced therapies
Future Directions
  • Need for prospective cohort studies with histological endpoints
  • Randomized controlled trials essential for MC treatment approval
  • Standardized outcome framework needed (regulatory approval)
  • Integration of clinical and molecular data for precision medicine

Characteristic Value
Female sex, n (%) 122 (85.9)
Age at MC diagnosis, median [IQR] 52.6 [38.3–61.7]
Age at advanced therapy start, median [IQR] 56.6 [44.3–65.3]
Collagenous colitis, n (%) 75 (52.8)
Lymphocytic colitis, n (%) 67 (47.2)
PPI use at diagnosis, n (%) 37 (26.1)
SSRI use at diagnosis, n (%) 37 (26.1)
Active smoking, n (%) 37 (26.2)
Former smoking, n (%) 19 (13.5)
Never smoked, n (%) 85 (60.3)
Familial MC history, n (%) 3 (2.1)
Concomitant Immune-Mediated Diseases
  • Celiac disease: 4 (2.8%)
  • Psoriasis: 9 (6.3%)
  • Spondyloarthropathy: 7 (4.9%)
  • Rheumatoid arthritis: 3 (2.1%)
  • Other: 8 (5.6%)

Definitions Used in Study
Clinical Response
  • ≥50% reduction in stool frequency from baseline
Clinical Remission (Hjortswang Criteria)
  • Mean <3 stools per day
  • OR <1 watery stool per day
Budesonide Dependency
  • Recurrence upon tapering/discontinuation → requires ongoing therapy
Budesonide Refractoriness
  • Primary non-response (PNR): no response to adequate induction
  • Secondary loss of response (LOR): initial response → subsequent failure
Treatment Cycle Definition
  • Initiation of a new advanced therapy in a given patient
  • Each drug change = distinct treatment cycle regardless of prior exposure
